data.title: Pfizer's Oral Obesity Drug Flop - A Symptom Of A Deeper Pipeline Problem
data.description:
Introduction

Pfizer (NYSE:PFE) suffered a setback in April after announcing plans to axe its oral obesity drug, danuflipron. The news comes after a trial patient experienced "potential drug-induced liver injury." Pfizer's once-daily, GLP-1 receptor agonist had a tricky developmental path before this latest development. Recall that Pfizer discontinued a twice-daily version of the pill back in December 2023 due to tolerability issues. Danuglipron was, arguably, Pfizer's highest profile experimental drug. It's back to the drawing board. Notably, Pfizer now shifts its focus to its Phase 2 oral GIPR antagonist drug.

The following article analyzes the implications of Pfizer's failure for both Pfizer and the overall pharmaceutical industry. I also take a look at Pfizer's current valuation, which seems reasonable in light of impending patent cliffs, meriting a Hold rating.

The $100 Billion Obesity Market Landscape

First, let's review the background of the obesity market, which is expected to eclipse $100 billion in the coming years. Novo Nordisk (NVO) and Eli Lilly (LLY) are the two big leaders now, with Wegovy and Zepbound, respectively. Wegovy was first to launch in the obesity market (June 2021), but Zepbound (approved in late 2023) is already fighting toe-to-toe due to its superiority in weight loss outcomes. Both are weekly injections. Despite their massive potential, they come with limitations (e.g., tolerability, compliance). Drugmakers are predominantly focused on different routes of administration (e.g., oral), tolerability profiles, and mechanisms of action (e.g., amylin).

Oral obesity treatments are interesting, especially for their potential application in weight loss maintenance.

Here are a few notable ones in development (data compiled from multiple sources). This is not intended to be an exhaustive list.

Company Drug MOA Phase Results Potential Approval
Novo Nordisk Oral semaglutide GLP-1 receptor agonist Phase 3 completed 15.1% weight loss at 68 weeks EU and U.S. regulatory filing imminent
Eli Lilly Orforglipron GLP-1 receptor agonist (non-peptide) Phase 3 At 36 weeks, achieved between 9.4% and 14.7% weight loss across different doses Potentially 2026
Structure Therapeutics GSBR-1290 GLP-1 receptor agonist (small molecule) Phase 2b Achieved 6.2% placebo-adjusted mean weight loss at 12 weeks Phase 2b data expected Q4 2025
Viking Therapeutics VK2735 GLP-1 & GIP receptor dual agonist Phase 2 Up to 8.2% (n=9) weight loss (6.8% placebo-adjusted) in the 28-day Phase 1 MAD trial Phase 2 VENTURE-Oral data expected in 2H 2025
Novo Nordisk Amycretin GLP-1 & amylin receptor dual agonist Phase 1 13.1% weight loss after 12 weeks in Phase 1 trial Novo is "very comfortable" with launch "within this decade"

The Oral Obesity Drug Challenge

Danuflipron's exit underscores some of the challenges associated with developing oral weight loss medications. Even if the drug avoids degradation from the stomach's acidic environment, it still needs to pass through the intestinal lining to enter the bloodstream. Once absorbed, it stops by the liver for "first-pass metabolism." This can further degrade the molecules before they reach their target receptors. Peptides like danuflipron and Viking Therapeutics' (VKTX) VK2735 are particularly vulnerable to these issues due to their large size. It is quite the journey, and getting it just right requires a lot of tinkering and good luck.

For instance, Rybelsus' (oral semaglutide approved for type 2 diabetes) users are instructed to take it at least 30 minutes before any food, water, or medicine with "no more than 4 oz plain water only." Oral semaglutide will have the same instructions once obesity is added to its label. While inconvenient, it beats liver injury!

Pfizer's Strategic Pivot Amid $19 Billion Patent Cliff

Danuflipron is just one of a few weight loss candidates in Pfizer's pipeline. PF-07976016 blocks glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide receptors (GIPR). GIP activity is mainly pancreatic, while GLP-1 activity is systemic. The hope is that targeting GIP receptors will cause fewer off-target effects throughout the body compared to GLP-1.

Meanwhile, PF-06954522 (oral) is a GLP-1R agonist in Phase 1 development for chronic weight management.

While Pfizer's dirt-cheap valuation (P/E under 8) and generous dividend yield of 7%-plus attract value investors, there are reasons for caution.

According to my dashboard, which isolates metrics I focus on when assessing biopharma stocks, Pfizer has many challenging ratios.

Now, many of these trends are due to Pfizer's legacy COVID products declining post-pandemic. The elephant in the room is "revenue at risk," which is estimated to be as high as 30% by 2028, with Eliquis, Prevnar, Ibrance, and Xtandi all losing their exclusivities around this time.

Drug Primary Indication 2023 Sales ($B) 2024 Sales ($B) Key Patent Expiry Year (U.S.)
Eliquis Atrial fibrillation, Thrombosis 12.2 (Global) 7.4 (Pfizer share) 2028
Prevnar Family Pneumonia Vaccine 6.44 6.41 2026
Ibrance Breast Cancer 4.75 4.37 2027
Xtandi Prostate Cancer 5.19 (Global) 2.04 (Pfizer share) 2027

This makes Pfizer's recent obesity setback all the more concerning. Some analysts had danuflipron generating over $2 billion in peak annual revenue. We must also consider that Pfizer is now years behind its oral competitors.

Judging by its stock performance (down 29% in the past five years), the market is not confident Pfizer will be able to "fill the shoes" of these blockbuster drugs.

In this vein, Pfizer's pipeline relies heavily on its $43 billion acquisition of Seagen in late 2023. In its fiscal year 2024, Seagen's portfolio brought in $3.4 billion in revenue. The hope is that its ADC-focused pipeline can generate as much as $20 billion in annual revenue by 2030, effectively replacing its at-risk blockbusters. But this is far from guaranteed.

Meanwhile, Pfizer is implementing operational efficiency initiatives, including $4.5 billion in target savings by the end of 2025, with a plan to earmark a portion of these savings for R&D and organizational restructuring next year.

Valuation Analysis and Sensitivity

Next, I conducted a discounted cash flow (DCF) analysis on PFE. My goal is to uncover growth and margin expectations implied by PFE's valuation.

Looking back, Pfizer's $63.627 billion in 2024 revenue represented 6.84% year-over-year growth. However, in Q1 2025, revenues of $13.715 billion represented a year-over-year decline of 7.8%. Notably, Pfizer's 2025 revenue prospects are impacted by the Inflation Reduction Act, or IRA, by an estimated $1 billion. Pfizer's EBITDA was $23.322 billion in 2024. Free cash flow (CFO less CapEx) was $9.835 billion (15.5% FCF margin).

Looking ahead, analysts believe Pfizer's revenues will retreat to the mid-$50 billion before inching back up.

As for inputs, I elected for -4% revenue "growth," 16% FCF margins, and a stock beta of 0.5. These are in line with current estimates and trends. Everything else is standard DCF methodology.

The projection is a bit more pessimistic than the analysts'. However, the terminal rate does imply the pharmaceutical giant returns to modest growth after nine years.

Looking at my sensitivity analysis, which also acts as a reverse DCF model, PFE is priced for -4.3% growth assuming stable FCF margins of 16%.

Given the upcoming patent cliffs, -4.3% growth is reasonable.

With that said, Pfizer does appear conservatively valued, trading relatively close to what, I believe, is a "worst case" scenario.

Conclusion

To wrap things up, while danuflipron's failure sends ripples in the obesity treatment pond, no boats are flipping over. To Pfizer's stock, this was a ho-hum event. It hardly budged. Maybe the writing was on the wall for danuflipron? Since my last look in July 2024, not much has changed for Pfizer to merit an adjustment to my Hold rating. With revenues from key drugs coming off the books in a few years, Pfizer appears increasingly ill-prepared to fill the voids. Then again, this is what it is already priced for. From my porch, Pfizer is appropriately valued. It's not a bargain, though. Notably, Pfizer is ripe for asymmetric upside in the event it overperforms by just a little.

data.title: Asset Entities: The First Publicly Traded Asset Management Bitcoin Treasury For $8 Per Share -- Strong Buy
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Asset Entities (NASDAQ:ASST) is a very volatile stock, trading at less than $8 last week, hitting $12 just a few days ago, and now hovering around $8.10 per share. Equally important, however, is that it's a stock with a lot of good news. I'm here to explain the risk-reward profile of Asset Entities and Strive Asset Management, which merged earlier this month. From where I'm standing, I think there's a lot more upside to this stock in the mid-term. Asset Entities is, according to their bio, "a technology company [that] provides social media marketing and content delivery services across Discord, TikTok, and other social media platforms." This means their revenue is pretty diversified, and they are expanding into a new area that I expect will bring substantial growth after the merger with Strive Asset Management, which is a subsidiary of Strive Enterprises, Inc. The reason is that these two are becoming a single entity that aims to be the first public Bitcoin Treasury Company.

Below is the post on X from Strive Asset Management's CFO, Ben Pham, emphasizing just how big a deal this is.

Before this merger deal, Asset Entities was recognized as the first publicly traded company centered around Discord communities. It has a flagship product called the "AE.360.DDM", which offers customers design, development, and management services for Discord servers. This kind of product caters to businesses, influencers, and educational communities, basically anyone looking to gain a heads-up on social media. Additionally, the company provides "Ternary", a Stripe-verified CRM and payment platform. They offer these services on other platforms like TikTok, Instagram, and X, not just Discord. Their financials, however, aren't the prettiest. Their Q1'25 revenue was $170,749, a 36.8% increase from $124,841 in Q1 2024. Operating Expenses were $1.83 million, up 20.9% year-over-year. Management states this is driven by higher advertising, marketing, payroll, and administrative costs, which makes sense for such a small company trying to grow.

The market already reacted pretty well to the merger when it happened back around May 7th, with the stock up 1,240% since then, which you can see clearly in the chart below of the stock against the S&P 500 over the past month.

But, this Tuesday the stock dropped almost 22%, and investors, seeing the volatility, are all wondering: Is this the time to buy the pullback or stay on the sidelines? So, I'm here to give my take, especially in light of the latest transitions underway, the main of which is the following:

[Strive Asset Management and Asset Entities] announced the signing of a $750 million private investment in public equity (PIPE), with an additional $750 million in potential financing available upon the exercise of warrants, which could increase total potential proceeds to $1.5 billion.

To put that into context, we're talking about a massive merger deal with the money coming from a group of private institutional investors. What this tells me is that there are some big-money players who are very confident in the future of the stock. Since this merger has taken another step in the right direction, securing a great amount of funds and directing those funds for growth, I will initiate my coverage of Asset Entities with a buy.

What's the new deal?

In early May, Strive Asset Management and Asset Entities Inc. announced their merger, causing the stock to skyrocket 1,320% over the past month, and today, May 28th, they secured $750 million in new funding. To put this in context, Asset Entities only made $633,000 in revenue for the whole of 2024. So, where's all this money coming from? Well, there's a group of investors operating through something called a Private Investment in Public Equity (PIPE). Now, think of a PIPE as a quick way for public companies to raise money from their private investors, and keep in mind these private investors aren't like you and me; they have millions to invest. In this case, these investors are buying Asset Entities' shares at $1.35 per share. That's good news for people like us, as it's 121% higher than what the stock was trading at before the merger announcement. What this tells me is that these investors have huge confidence in this new deal. On top of this, there's also the potential for another $750 million to come in if investors choose to exercise warrants. Now, these warrants are like options to buy more shares later, which would bring the total investment to $1.5 billion. If that happens, you should buckle your seatbelts.

So why the need to raise all these funds? Strive Asset Management is planning on merging with Asset Entities Inc. to create a new kind of public company: one focused on a few new money-making ideas. The $750 million is set to fund the merged entity's new strategic plan, to buy distressed Bitcoin assets as well as scoop up any undervalued biotech firms. Matt Cole, CEO of Strive Asset, seems bullish, saying traditional Bitcoin investors rely mainly on price ratios like price-to-NAV. But he's looking to head in a new direction when it comes to valuing Bitcoin investments. These new methods are said to be based on fundamentals and smart acquisitions, not just tracking Bitcoin's price. Since the PIPE is injecting all this cash, the new entity has no debt; if it stumbles across a struggling bitcoin company, it can move in quickly. What this means for investors is that Asset Entities is essentially expanding its revenue stream. It's no longer just a social/digital media tech business, but now has a financial engineering play in crypto and special situations investing.

Expect Volatility

Since these new PIPE shares come from institutional players, they sweep in and buy shares at a discounted price, in this case $1.35 a share. This is a 121% premium to Asset Entities' pre-merger price, but there's a catch. These shares will be restricted at first and can't be sold by the investors straight away, there's going to be some kind of lockup period on the shares, although we don't know exactly how long this is. In my opinion and historically, this lock-up period on the shares should be at least 3 months. So we can expect some stock volatility in that period but an overall upward trend. My reasoning for this is simple: the stock is up 1,335% over the past month, there's huge momentum here, and I expect it to keep going off the back of the new funds secured. On top of this, yesterday we saw a 21% drop in the stock price, which, I believe, is due to investors not fully understanding what this deal means for Asset Management. Also, I know what you're thinking: can those institutional investors sell off their shares immediately after that lock-up period is over? Well, the answer is, they can. However, they have at least a 3-month period in which they're bullish on the stock going up, so why can we jump on that train too? This also works as a short-term investment for me with a big upside.

A Rollercoaster Ride This May

So, a month ago, this was just a penny stock, trading at $0.63, and has since hit a high of $13 per share. The good news of the merger got well priced into the stock, and then between May 9th and May 14th, things calmed down with the stock dropping 40%. I think investors started reaping profits and selling off their shares. At this point, the stock started facing resistance around $5; investors knew there was something more here. At this point, only the news of the merger was out, and the funds for buying Bitcoin had not yet been secured. Despite the sell-off on May 9th, the stock pulled through, skyrocketing another 176%, hitting a high of $13 last Thursday. I'm bullish because the stock has fallen off 40% since that high, and you can pick up shares for $8. What makes me particularly bullish is this new bit of news I've just explained: they've secured the funding. I don't think investors fully understand what this PIPE investment does for Asset Entities. What it does is show huge confidence in their new entity to buy Bitcoin and make a huge upside. In the short term, I expect the stock to shoot up and recover to that $12-13 range it was before.

Valuation

The market cap is at $129 million, there's been a 52-week low of $0.34 and a high of $13, which goes to show you really how volatile this stock is right now. Because Asset Entities is expanding into Bitcoin and other revenue streams, it is hard to place them among competitors. Seeking Alpha compares them to peers such as Cardlytics Inc. (CDLX) and Marchex Inc. (MCHX), both advertising and analytics tech companies. These would be accurate peers before this merger, but now Asset Entities finds itself in a new ballgame. The RSI right now is at 53, and on its way down fast. This is the time to buy for investors, in my opinion. I think the $8 range is a good entry point for those looking to make a quick upside on the stock.

What's Next

Investors should be following the stock daily; we should see an upside this week from the news that funds have been raised for the new merger, and the stock is already showing signs, up 13% on Thursday so far. Those institutional investors are bullish, and so am I. Over the next few months, keep a lookout for any news on Asset Entities acquiring small biotech firms and any big Bitcoin investments. These will eventually be the new catalysts going forward.

In terms of individual stock positive contributors, IHS Towers, the African tower company, reported good quarterly earnings that suggest it is through the worst of the disruption in Nigeria and is more confident about an increase in telecommunications operators’ capex, following the government’s decision to allow them to raise prices.

Eiffage (OTCPK:EFGSF)(OTCPK:EFGSY), the European toll road operator and construction company, reacted positively to news that Germany would introduce a €500 billion infrastructure spending plan, with investors also hopeful that this would be the first of many such stimulus plans in Europe.

RWE (OTC:RWNEF), the European electric utility, benefited from a combination of the German stimulus bill, which provides funding for new generation, and the company’s announcement of cuts to renewable capex, which will allow further returns of capital to shareholders.

In terms of individual stock detractors, XPLR Infrastructure (XIFR), the US renewable developer, announced the outcome of its strategic review, which included suspending the dividend and issuing guidance that there will be no growth through to 2030. While we had expected a dividend cut and some paring back of growth, the outcome was more severe than anticipated, with investors reacting very negatively to the news.

EDP Renováveis (OTCPK:EDRVF)(OTCPK:EDRVY), the European renewable developer, moved lower throughout the quarter as investors remained concerned about potential cuts to the Inflation Reduction Act in the US, as well as a weakening power price environment in Europe driven by lower gas prices.

Mobico Group (OTCPK:NXPGF), the UK transportation company, fell as investors grew impatient with the lack of news on the US school bus sale, with initial expectations that a deal would be announced by the end of 2024. The company has been limited in what it can say, other than confirming that if the sale were not happening, it would be required to notify investors — which gives us confidence that negotiations are still ongoing.

Market review

Global equity markets ended lower over the quarter. Investors were initially optimistic about stronger economic growth after US President Trump’s inauguration, as the prospects of lower taxes, deregulation and higher fiscal spending helped ease recession concerns – a view supported by resilient economic data. Meanwhile, continued disinflation had prompted the US Federal Reserve (Fed), European Central Bank and Bank of England to cut interest rates from mid-2024. However, since the start of 2025, investors began to anticipate a more measured pace of easing, as central banks maintained a data-dependent stance. In particular, some of President Trump’s policies, including increased tariffs and reduced immigration were seen as potentially inflationary. As the quarter progressed, concerns grew over the economic effects of these tariff policies amid fears of a global trade war. Early signs suggested that tariff-driven inflationary pressures were weighing on consumer sentiment and could slow US economic growth. Simultaneously, stubborn inflation fuelled worries that the Fed might moderate its rate cuts despite weakening data, given its relatively hawkish stance.

Outlook & strategy

The tariff announcements proved more detrimental to growth and more inflationary than expected. Given that tariffs now appear as a more concrete policy measure for the US – as opposed to representing a negotiating tool – investors’ reaction is reflective of a broad-based downward repricing of global growth. This highlights that investors are most concerned about the impact on US economic growth, through a combination of demand destruction (caused by higher retail prices) and lower investment spending (caused by corporate uncertainty). Some initial estimates from investment banks and our own macroeconomic research outlook point to a 1–2% hit to GDP. The speed at which we have moved from a market characterised by rises in US stocks, Treasury yields and the US dollar is remarkable.

We remain positive on global equities, particularly for our highly selective, quality-first approach. In an environment characterised by slowing growth, heightened volatility and rising dispersion, we believe that high-quality companies that enjoy robust financial positions and structural growth tailwinds are particularly well placed.

With the recent implementation of tariffs, investors may be concerned about potential market disruptions. However, infrastructure remains one of the most resilient asset classes, offering stable and predictable cash flows largely insulated from trade tensions. Many key infrastructure sectors, including utilities, telecommunications towers and natural gas pipelines, operate under regulated or contracted revenue models, making them less exposed to tariff-driven cost fluctuations. As a result, infrastructure continues to stand out as an attractive investment opportunity, providing defensive characteristics and long-term value in an uncertain macroeconomic environment. Now more than ever, we believe infrastructure offers a compelling way to navigate volatility while benefiting from essential, irreplaceable assets. Amid the current focus on near-term trends, it is important not to lose sight of the long-term structural drivers we see in infrastructure — namely the energy transition, digital acceleration, ageing infrastructure and increasing urbanisation.

data.title: Seagate Technology Holdings: One Of The Rare 'Too Good To Be True' Opportunities
data.description:
It's hard these days to find cheap companies with a great story. If someone told me that in 2025, there's a company with a PEG under 1, double-digit growth, and an innovative product with low competition - I'd say it's too good to be true. But this is the situation with Seagate (STX). The stock is testing its all-time high from 2022, and it's no coincidence. With leadership and innovation in the HDDs market, which is considered a duopoly market, attractive valuation, and optimistic forecasts. I think Seagate is a strong buy.

The Rise Of The Data

Seagate is a data storage company specializing in two main segments: Hard Disk Drives (HDDs) and Solid-State Drives (SSDs). In general, Seagate - and the data storage industry as a whole benefits from the rapidly growing demand for data storage, driven by the exponential increase in data, especially due to the AI revolution. Models like GPT are consuming trillions of words, pictures, and videos for training. In addition, the high quality of video content in 4K, and soon in 8K, dramatically increases the size of the files. Adding to that, the IT revolution has created billions of objects such as cars, cameras, and sensors that generate real-time data which needs to be analyzed and stored. According to ComplexDiscovery, global data volume is expected to more than double between 2024 and 2029, growing from approximately 149 zettabytes to over 550 zettabytes.

Seagate stands to benefit not only from the volume growth in global data but also from the strategic positioning of HDDs in data center architecture. While SSDs are faster, hard drives still store about 90% of the data in large data centers because they offer much more storage at a lower cost. Big tech companies like Google use a mix of both SSDs for quick access and hard drives for storing large amounts of data.

The Chief Executive Officer Dave Mosley said during the last earnings call:

In the March quarter, cloud nearline revenue and exabytes were up by nearly 10% sequentially, almost doubling year-over-year amid a very tight supply environment. The growing demand for mass capacity storage aligns with the cloud CapEx investment cycle and ongoing build out of data center infrastructure to support AI transformations.

HAMR: A Game-Changing Innovation

Till here, cool, nice - a storage company that benefits from the exponential growth of data in the world. But there is more, a lot more. The HAMR technology (Heat-Assisted Magnetic Recording) is the game changer here.

HAMR is a revolution in the HDD field and is the main driver of Seagate's growth in the next few years. The technology is designed to dramatically increase the storage density of hard drives, in other words, to store much more data on each physical disk. Seagate is the primary innovator behind HAMR.

Seagate began developing HAMR more than a decade ago, investing heavily in R&D, while other companies explored alternative paths such as MAMR (Microwave-Assisted Magnetic Recording).

The Chief Executive Officer Dave Mosley said during the last earnings call:

HAMR is a groundbreaking technology that will extend TCO advantages for hard drives over other storage media for many years to come.

Seagate's competitor in this field is Western Digital (WDC). While both Seagate and Western Digital are investing heavily in next-generation HDD technologies, recent earnings calls make it clear that Seagate is the real leader in the HAMR. Seagate has already begun volume shipments to hyperscale customers and expects accelerated adoption in the second half of 2025. As CEO Dave Mosley stated in the last earnings call:

It is becoming increasingly evident to both our customers and the market that HAMR is a groundbreaking technology that will extend TCO advantages for hard drives over other storage media for many years to come.

Western Digital remains at least 18-24 months behind. The company is still in the engineering sample stage with only two hyperscale customers, and their CEO, Irving Tan, confirmed that in the last earning call:

We are looking to start qualification in the second half of calendar year 2026 and then ramping up production at scale in the first half of calendar year 2027.

While Western Digital is lagging, Gianluca Romano, Seagate's CFO, specifically attributed the increase last quarter to HAMR shipments:

Compared to the midpoint of our guidance, I would say that is actually coming from more volume on HAMR products.

Based on this, we can estimate that Seagate has a significant advantage in the HDD market, which could benefit the company in the next few years.

Numbers And Valuation:

So after we saw a great story, a leading product, and a significantly competitive advantage, you probably expect a high multiple.

Surprise: the company's P/E is 17.89 compared to peers at 21.20, and the forward P/E is 14.97 compared to peers at 21.74. The forward EV/EBITDA is 12.54 compared to peers at 13.83. The price-to-cash-flow ratio stands at 24.64, which is high, but expected to drop dramatically to a forward price-to-cash-flow of 17.24.

The company reported $2.16 billion in revenue in FQ3 2025 - a 30% YoY growth, and EPS of 1.90. I suggest looking at the gross margin trend, which grew from 10% in FQ1 2024 to 35% in the last quarter.

The company holds $800 million in cash and has $5 billion in debt. While this could be considered a risk, the company generated positive $259 million in cash flow from operations and a FCF $216 million in the last quarter.

Risks

In March 2025, there was an article in the WSJ that, I think, is worth noting:

Hard disk drive makers, under pressure from faster rivals, hope a breakthrough will keep it the dominant data-storage medium.

Seagate faces growing risks as SSD continue to gain market share due to their faster performance, lower power consumption, and narrowing cost gap with HDDs. The physical limitations of HDD technology, along with the increasing preference for SSDs in data centers, put pressure on Seagate's core business. While the company is investing heavily in HAMR and has secured large orders from major cloud customers, these advantages could be temporary. The global expansion of SSD manufacturing and Seagate's reliance on a small number of key clients further heighten its long-term risk profile. In the article, John Colgrove, founder and chief visionary officer of Pure Storage said:

The debate isn't will hard drives go away-it's when will they go away

I think this statement is a bit extreme. It is possible that the hard disk will definitely disappear, but it will take a long time, and it is a topic that is still too early to talk about.

In addition, there are a number of other risks to consider, including high exposure to cyclical demand from hyperscale providers, which makes its revenue sensitive to changes in cloud CAPEX. The company is also exposed to potential geopolitical and trade disruptions due to its global manufacturing. Along with high debt levels, that could become a problem if interest rates remain high for an extended period.

Summary

I think that despite the risks, Seagate's story of combining innovation and leadership with low competition combined with attractive valuation points to a positive future in the coming years, and therefore, in my opinion, Seagate may be one of the rare "too good to be true" opportunities in the market.

data.title: 2 Preferreds For A Stress-Free Retirement; Up To 8.7% Yield
data.description:
Co-authored with Hidden Opportunities

Life has an interesting rhythm. When we are younger, we often desire to travel the world and experience many things, but we often lack the financial means to pursue them. During our adult life, we have the financial means for the above, but we often lack the time, as we have kids' schedules, family responsibilities, and the fact that employment often comes with limited vacation days.

Time is life's most limited resource - no fortune can buy an extra second. This is why, when we retire, we should spend our time doing things we enjoy, rather than being obsessed with what is happening in the markets. Retirement often marks a time when you have the opportunity to have the time and the financial means to pursue your passions.

At High Dividend Opportunities, we believe in using dividends as a means to build financial power in our retirement, allowing the capital markets to reward us for simply participating. Preferred securities enjoy a placement higher up in the corporate structure of the issuers, and as such, they enjoy preferential treatment when it comes to receiving a share of the company's profits. They are less affected by the everyday news surrounding the industry and continue to pay generous dividends for years (if not decades). Without further ado, let's dive into our favorite perpetual preferreds.

Pick #1: TDS Preferreds - Up To 8.7% Yields

Telephone and Data Systems Inc. (TDS) is a Fortune 1000 company that provides wireless and broadband services to ~6 million connections nationwide, through its operating subsidiaries. The company primarily maintains a strong wireless and fiber footprint in rural regions of the country. TDS reported its Q1 earnings on May 2nd. The company deployed 14,000 new marketable fiber addresses and remains on track for its annual goal of 150,000. TDS notably delivered a 2% YoY increase in the revenue per connection to $65.67.

The most important component of the earnings call was the discussion around the proposed transaction between T-Mobile and US Cellular, which is expected to close in the next few months. Following the completion, it is expected that United States Cellular (USM) will declare a special dividend to shareholders, which will result in TDS receiving its pro rata share (being an 83% shareholder). TDS plans to use the funds to repay substantially all its bank debt, totalling ~$1.2 billion at the end of Q1. If there are proceeds from the secondary USM transactions with AT&T and Verizon, worth $1 billion each, the company will prioritize funding and advancing its fiber program and evaluate opportunities to return to shareholders. Management was vocal about not looking to redeem the preferreds, which become callable next year.

"These are perpetual preferred stock. They're a nice foundational capital going forward that we intend to keep in place as we think about." - Vicki Villacrez, EVP and CFO

TDS also disclosed its transformation program that is aimed at achieving $100 million in cost savings by the end of 2028. Some savings are expected in 2025, with the company working to staff internal construction crews for fiber builds for one-third of builds during the year, and this comes at a significantly lower average cost than external contractors.

TDS has affirmed its 2025 guidance, projecting Adj. EBITDA between $320 - $360 million, and CapEx between $375 - $425 million. About 80% of this CapEx amount is dedicated to fiber deployment, primarily in expansion markets and for maintaining/enhancing existing infrastructure. The company spent $17 million on preferred dividends during Q1 ($68 million annualized) and had ~124 million in cash and cash equivalents at the end of the quarter.

What do the Q1 notes from performance and management comments mean for preferred shareholders? With the company's debt getting out of the way, the preferreds gain prominence in the capital structure. However, management was quite clear about their intention not to call these securities for the foreseeable future. These are perpetual preferreds, and we have them in our portfolio with the intention to collect income for the foreseeable future. As such, management's comments do not concern us. Not very long ago, both preferreds were trading at 50 cents on the dollar, with the market irrationally fearing the worst, with limited data pointing to the same. But valuable assets have significant power, and USM has proved to be that, with the potential to generate proceeds that will reshape the future of TDS.

This continues to emphasize the need to take data-driven decisions and the benefit of being a buyer of discounted income amidst market pessimism.

  • 6.625% Deposit Shares, Series UU Cumulative Redeemable Perpetual Preferred (TDS.PR.U) - Yield 8.6%

  • 6.00% Deposit Shares, Series VV Cumulative Redeemable Perpetual Preferred (TDS.PR.V) - Yield 8.7%

TDS preferreds continue to provide attractive yields, with TDS-V currently presenting an 8.7% QDI opportunity, with ~45% upside to par value

Pick #2: GOOD Preferreds - Up To 7.5% Yields

Gladstone Commercial Corporation (GOOD) is an industrial REIT with a portfolio of 141 properties, leased to 107 tenants across 20 industries. The portfolio's leases had an average remaining term of 6.8 years, offering long-term income visibility. Notably, ~53% of tenants hold an investment grade or investment grade-equivalent credit rating, supporting the stability and resilience of cash flows. The company reported its Q1 earnings on May 7th, reporting core FFO of $0.34/share, flat sequentially, and covering its quarterly dividend of $0.30/share. During the three months of 2025, the REIT collected 100% of all outstanding base rents and ended the quarter

During the quarter, the company was active in acquisitions, purchasing six industrial properties in Texas, including a five-property industrial portfolio in Houston for $29.3 million with a 10-year lease term, and one industrial property in Dallas-Fort Worth for $44.0 million with an 11.3-year lease term. The total capital deployed for these industrial acquisitions was over $73 million, aimed at increasing the REIT's industrial concentration to position itself for the industrial shift from the U.S. trade policy.

GOOD reported a 6.6% YoY increase in same-store rents, and the REIT sold one office property for a gain of $377,000. GOOD maintains a 98.4% occupancy of $80.5 million in total liquidity (including $10.4 million in cash balance) with only 2.3% of its total debt maturing this year. Just 1.9% of the annualized base rent faces lease maturity this year.

  • 6.625% Series E Cumulative Redeemable Preferred Stock (GOODN) - Yield 7.5%

  • 6.00% Series G Cumulative Redeemable Preferred Stock (GOODO) - Yield 7.5%

GOOD's perpetual preferreds continue to present attractive investments that pay generous monthly dividends. GOODO currently offers a 7.5% yield and ~24% upside to par.

Conclusion

Retirement isn't just the end of a career - it's the beginning of a chapter where time becomes your most valuable asset, and you have the flexibility in your schedule to accommodate the things you desire. Having a strategy that pays you every day without having to focus on the headlines and timing the sale of your holdings will provide you with the time and the mental liberation you deserve.

By focusing on reliable income from preferred securities, you can turn your portfolio into a source of stability. Your entire adult life was spent working hard to earn your money. It is imperative that you train this money to work for you, so you can focus on what matters most: living fully, without financial worry. This is the power of income investing.

data.title: Abercrombie & Fitch's Trough May Already Be Here -- Further Recovery Possible
data.description:
The Worst May Already Be Baked In, As The Bulls Defend ANF's Recent Recovery

We previously covered Abercrombie & Fitch Co. (NYSE:ANF) in March 2025, discussing its drastic sell-off then, partly attributed to the intensified tariff war and potential recessionary risks, with it likely impacting its near-term top/ bottom-line performance.

Despite the management's underwhelming FY2025 guidance, we had believed that it remained well positioned to weather the near-term uncertainties, thanks to the rich Free Cash Flow generation and the healthier balance sheet, significantly aided by the overly discounted valuations.

ANF 1Y Stock Price

Since then, ANF had already further pulled back to chart new lows, albeit notably recovering after the new US administration announced a 90-day tariff pause by early April 2025, with a similar recovery also observed in its retail peers in varying degrees.

Even so, there is no denying the impacted consumer spending trends, attributed to the elevated inflationary pressure, the heightened downturn risks, and the expensive borrowing costs, directly contributing to the Americas region's underperformance thus far.

This is with +4% YoY in comparable sales growth in FQ1'25 and Abercrombie at -10% YoY, notably decelerating compared to FY2024 growth levels at +17% YoY/ +16% YoY and FQ1'24 levels at +21% YoY/ +29% YoY, respectively.

Given that the region comprises 79.7% of its overall revenues (-0.6 points YoY) and the brand at 49.9% (-6 points YoY), it is unsurprising that ANF has already reported an impacted FQ1'25 performance.

This is with revenues of $1.09B (-31% QoQ/ +6.8% YoY), operating margins of 9.3% (-6.9 points QoQ/ -3.4 YoY), and adj EPS of $1.59 (-55.4% QoQ/ -25.7% YoY), albeit notably beating the consensus' bearish estimates.

The uncertainties have also triggered ANF's revised FY2025 guidance, with higher net sales at +4.5% YoY at the midpoint (compared to a year ago at +16%), lower operating margins at 13% (-2 points YoY), and lower adj EPS at $10 (-6.4% YoY).

This is compared to the prior guidance at +4% YoY, 14.5% (-0.5 points YoY), and $10.90 (+1.9% YoY), respectively, with it underscoring the accelerating demand for Hollister at +22% YoY in FQ1'25, building upon the +19% observed in FY2024 and +13% in FQ1'24.

This is albeit negated by the ongoing trade war at an approximate cost impact of around $50M, based on the 10% tariff on all global imports into the US and 30% tariff on imports from China.

This is on top of the lower Average Unit Retail attributed to the seasonal carry-over inventory clearance in FQ1'25, compared to its "clean inventory position" in FQ1'24, with moderate gross margins likely in FQ2'25.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

These reasons may also be why the consensus has already downgraded their forward estimates, with ANF expected to report a decelerating top/ bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +2.9%/ -3% through FY2027.

This is compared to the original estimates of +4.5%/ +11.8%, the 5Y historical growth at +6.4%/ +71.1%, and 10Y historical growth at +2.8%/ +21.4%, respectively, as its growth notably stalls during the ongoing macroeconomic uncertainties.

ANF Valuations

On the other hand, we believe that ANF continues to appear compelling at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 7.39x (pre-market), down from its 1Y mean of 12.71x, 5Y mean of 29.60x, and 10Y mean of 20.21x, while seemingly finding a floor at the April 2025 bottom of 6.18x.

Despite the supposedly flattish adj EPS growth projections over the next few years, we believe that the worst may already be here, as the trade war seemingly de-escalates and the US inflation cools, albeit partly negated by the Fed's pessimistic meeting minutes.

Nonetheless, there is no denying ANF's overly discounted valuations when compared against its US-based Consumer Apparel Retail peers, including:

  • American Eagle Outfitters, Inc. (AEO) at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 11.22x with a consensus adj EPS growth estimates at a 3Y CAGR of -4.9%,
  • The Gap, Inc. (GPS) at 12.19x/ +5.2%,
  • Urban Outfitters, Inc. (URBN) at 15.05x/ +12.2%, respectively,

With it implying an excellent margin of safety for those looking at the dollar cost average.

So, Is ANF Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

ANF 5Y Stock Price

For now, ANF has already lost -53.4% or the equivalent -$5.85B of its market cap since the 2024 peaks, albeit seemingly finding a floor at the recent April 2025 bottom of $65.40, as the bulls also defend its recent recovery by +34% to retest the next resistance levels of $80s ranges.

It is apparent that the stock is trading at a slight premium to our bear-case fair value estimates of $66.00, based on the FY2024 adj EPS of $10.69 and the April 2025 P/E bottom of 6.18x.

On the other hand, assuming an upward re-rating to ANF's 1Y P/E mean of 12.71x (nearer to its peer group mean of 12.46x), there may be an excellent upside potential of +54.9% to our base-case fair value estimates of $135.80.

Assuming that the tariff headwinds lift and the retailer's adj EPS growth materializes in the double digits (similar to its 5Y and 10Y historical performance), there is no saying that the stock may be upgraded to its prior 10Y P/E mean of ~20x as well, with it likely to trigger a more than doubling upside potential to our bull-case fair value estimates of $213.80.

Despite the intermediate-term tariff headwinds, ANF remains well capitalized in the weather the uncertainties as well, as observed in the healthy balance sheet with a net cash position of $607.56M (-31.6% QoQ/ -6.6% YoY) and zero debts.

This is on top of the management's reiterated FY2025 share repurchase guidance at $400M, as they expect accelerated shareholder returns with around 49M in diluted weighted average shares by the end of the year (down from the prior guidance of 51M and a year ago at 52.46M).

These build upon the immense value arising from the ongoing share retirement, with -4.9% of its float already retired over the last twelve months and -21.1% since FY2019, as the management recognizes the stock's overly discounted valuations and the oversold position at current levels.

Combined with the materialization of the floor during the worst of the April 2025 meltdown, we are reiterating our Buy rating for the ANF stock.

Given the notable one-day rally of +14%, interested readers may want to observe the stock price movement for a little longer, attributed to the potential profit-taking and volatility arising from the higher short interest volumes by +28.6% on a YoY basis.

Combined with the intermediate-term tariff uncertainties and the rather greedy stock market sentiments, some patience may be warranted indeed.

data.title: Apple: Double-Down When The Market Gets Silly
data.description:
Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) has been struggling lately. With shares worth barely $200 as I write this sentence, this stock that has historically returned 26% per year in the iPhone era (i.e., since 2007) is still a solid 20%-plus below the all-time highs reached in December 2024.

I can understand why traders and investors might be reluctant to go all-in on the Cupertino company at this moment. An environment of higher import fees that could hurt margins, sticky interest rates, and uncertainty around consumer confidence and spending could be harmful to Apple. At the same time, AAPL stock has proven multiple times that it can be a great addition to a portfolio when bought on relative weakness, as is the case now.

Long-time winner is lagging most peers now

Compared to its Magnificent Seven peer group (MAGS), Apple stock has performed worse than all but one peer — namely Alphabet (GOOG)(GOOGL), see chart below — over the past year. The underperformance was most notable since the now-infamous "Liberation Day" selloff of early April, considering that AAPL had only been trailing Meta (META) and Tesla (TSLA) through the end of March.

Since May 2012, when Meta went public as Facebook, an interesting pattern could be observed among the five "FAANG" (a.k.a. old-school Big Tech) stocks:

  1. Losers, defined here as shares that underperformed the peer group the most over a rolling 12-month period, tend to outperform the peer group over the following 12 months in "mean reversion" fashion
  2. AAPL has been a top-performing stock 35% of the time, using month-end prices, which is more than the 20% that one would expect of each of the five stocks, were there not any outperformance bias

Regarding the first point above, the chart below shows the pattern between Apple's trailing 12-month returns and forward 12-month returns. The inverse relationship (granted, with a low r-squared, after all, generating alpha in the markets is not that straightforward) suggests that Apple stock tends to do well following a period of underperformance.

The above suggests that Apple, a Big Tech loser in the May 2024 to May 2025 period, could be ripe for a rebound relative to its peers.

Exaggerated fears over trade war

Regarding business fundamentals, I believe that the biggest overhang that discourages investors from owning AAPL today is the "US vs. the World" trade war and its implications. And, as I mentioned above, I can appreciate the bearish (or at least cautious) thesis.

In the end, Apple is a discretionary consumer product and service provider that sells tech items at a premium price. While consumer confidence rebounded in the most recent print, the index remains remarkably close to the ten-year lows reached in 2020. To make matters worse, Apple was recently the target of a tariff threat by the US President, who suggested that a 25% tax be paid on iPhones imported into the US from any foreign country.

Still, I think that the fears may be overblown and might have served to offer investors a slightly lower entry point into the stock. On the tariff threat, Mr. Trump has proven to be quite flexible on his proposed policies, with the potential iPhone tariff presented via the President's social media channel sounding more like an attempt to nudge the Cupertino company into acting in the President's interest.

Second, led by an operations-minded CEO in Tim Cook, Apple has been remarkable at navigating supply channel issues in the past while maintaining pricing power and (modestly) growing sales volume through less-than-ideal business environments. Apple did an amazing job diversifying production outside of China (see pie chart below), a country that not too long ago was home to nearly half of Apple's largest global suppliers, following the 2018-2019 trade war between the Asian country and the US.

Also worth noting, 75% of Apple's fiscal 2024 revenues came from the products segment, which, I believe, is most vulnerable to tariff threats. However, about 40% of the company's operating profit comes from services (with the biggest drivers likely being the App Store and the licensing deal with Alphabet), which I believe to be much less exposed to trade war matters.

How about rich valuations?

A common pushback that I often hear from Apple bears is the rich valuation. Once upon a time, right before CEO Tim Cook took over, AAPL traded at an earnings multiple of around 12x. As recently as the beginning of calendar 2025, that number reached 36x on a forward basis (see chart below, purple line), and even higher shortly after the initial impact of the COVID-19 crisis.

First, I think that skepticism over rich valuations is a fair concern, and I wouldn't want to play it down much. However, it strikes me as a potential opportunity that AAPL's current-year P/E has decreased sharply from 36x only about five months ago to 28x today, and only 26x on a next-year basis. While long-term EPS growth expectations are not sizzling hot, at nearly 11% (blue line above), they are far from mediocre.

Second, and maybe even more importantly, I think that AAPL deserves to trade at a rich multiple for one key reason: relatively low risk of underperformance. Subjectively, Apple's brand appreciation and customer loyalty (in part supported by the strength of the ecosystem) are noteworthy bullish factors, in my view. Quantitatively, I believe that Apple's service business alone continues to justify a fair market value of $2 trillion, as I explained in detail recently. Today, Apple's entire equity is worth a hair less than $3 trillion.

When to double down on AAPL

Going back to the title of this article, I believe that Apple stock being down more than 20% from its peak, when most of FAANG ex-Alphabet are inches away from reclaiming all-time highs, is "silly". In my view, fears over owning AAPL today are overstated and, given enough time, the stock should become a peer group outperformer once again.

Of course, I could always be wrong in my bullishness. To mitigate this risk, I believe that using volatility and price levels as buying indicators (i.e., owning AAPL only when the former is low and the latter is high relative to a long-term moving average) could be a great idea. I discussed the approach in detail when I last covered Nvidia (NVDA).

The more sophisticated investor could even use a leveraged approach to benefit from a rebound in Apple stock without necessarily partaking in continued weakness in share price, should this occur. Applying the "managed risk" approach mentioned above to a leveraged Apple ETF, like the Direxion Daily AAPL Bull 2X Shares ETF (AAPU), would have produced outsized returns since 1994, as my back test below suggests:

The above is not meant to encourage most investors to "double down" on AAPL at current levels, necessarily. First, being twice leveraged AAPL could cause a substantial loss of portfolio value if the strategy is not diligently monitored often, ideally daily. Also, when a stock price declines and volatility rises while the leveraged position is still open, volatility drag could eat away at returns and result in performance that could fall well below expectations.

Of course, a "managed risk" strategy means being de-risked or even out of the trade altogether when volatility rises and price levels drop, which helps to reduce the risk of large losses in a leveraged play. But keep in mind that the use of leverage, even when it is properly monitored, can still produce large intraday swings at least. I believe that leveraged ETFs should be handled by more sophisticated investors who have the time and resources to track the portfolio performance daily and have a clear plan of action when things go sour. For more information on leveraged investing, check the guidelines offered by FINRA or the SEC.

Most importantly, the above merely serves as an illustration that large returns can be produced in Apple (or at least history suggests this to be the case) when a few simple rules are observed: own Apple (even on some leverage, if desired) following periods of weakness, especially after the stock has recovered from a period of outsized volatility and down-trending share price. This set of rules suggests that right about now might be a good time to be an Apple bull.

data.title: Vanadium Miners News For The Month Of May 2025
data.description:
Welcome to the Vanadium miners news.

May saw China & Europe spot vanadium pentoxide prices generally flat (slightly higher in China) and ferrovanadium prices slightly lower for the past month.

Vanadium uses

Vanadium is traditionally used to harden steel. Chinese rebar standards are requiring more vanadium. Also, Vanadium Flow Batteries [VRFBs] are becoming increasingly popular, especially for commercial energy storage, most notably in China. Vanadium Pentoxide [V2O5] is used in VRFBs and Ferrovanadium [FeV] is used in the steel industry.

Vanadium spot price history

As shown below, Europe V2O5 and China V2O5 prices were flat and slightly higher the last month.

Europe & China Vanadium Pentoxide [V2O5] Flake 98% one year price chart - Europe = USD 5.35/lb, China = USD 4.10/lb

As of May 27, 2025, Argus Media reports the China ferrovanadium (80%V) FOB price at US$23.90/kg, slightly lower for the past month.

Europe and China Ferrovanadium [FeV] 80% one year price chart - Europe = USD 24.20/kg, China = USD 23.90/kg

Vanadium demand versus supply

An April 2021 Wood Mackenzie report stated:

Global energy storage deployment surged a remarkable 62% in 2020, with 5 GW/9 GWh of new capacity added. This brought the total energy storage market to more than 27 GWh. Furthermore, we expect the global (energy storage) market to grow 27-fold by 2030.

ResearchAndMarkets forecasts the global VRFB market to grow from US$298m in 2023 to US$921m in 2028, at a CAGR of 20.5% (as of January 2023) (source)

Global VRFB forecast growth by region 2022-2031

CRU forecasts vanadium demand to double by 2032 mostly due to VRFB's (source) (As of January 2023)

Large scale global deployments of VRFB's are becoming more common - chart sourced as of April 2025 (source)

Vanadium market news

In May Vanitec reported numerous stories about new VRFB energy storage projects in China. These include an 80MW/320MWh VRFB project in Heyang County, a 250 kW/1 MWh VRFB demonstration project, a Vanadium Electrolyte Project in Panzhihua, commissioning of a 200MW/800MWh VRFB project in Qinghai, a 100MW/200MWh hybrid storage project (LFP & VRFB) in Yunnan, and a 100MW/400MWh VRFB project in Changzhou.

On May 14, Largo Inc.'s Vanadium Market update stated:

Vanadium markets in Europe and China remain weak, pressured by low steel and infrastructure demand and oversupply from Chinese and Russian producers, though aerospace demand is expected to pick up in the second half of 2025.

U.S. ferrovanadium ("FeV") prices are holding at levels approximately 9% higher than at the start of 2025, supported by increased buying interest amid geopolitical tensions and policy shifts that have tightened supply dynamics.

On May 23 Vanitec reported: "Dingbian County and China Sodium Energy sign RMB 970 Million agreement for new energy industrial park. Of interest is that China Sodium Energy plan to build a production base with an annual output capacity of 500 MWh of vanadium redox flow battery (VFB) systems."

On May 27 Vanitec reported:

Tyfast and US Vanadium sign MOU to advance industrial-scale production of lithium vanadium oxide anodes for heavy-duty EV batteries.

Note: U.S. Vanadium LLC is a private company.

Vanadium miner news

Vanadium producers

Glencore [LSX:GLEN] [HK:805] (OTCPK:GLCNF)

Glencore is a large vanadium producer, but vanadium production represents only a small portion of their revenue.

On April 30 Glencore announced: "First Quarter Production Report 2025." Glencore's Q1 2025 vanadium pentoxide production was 4.9mlb, down 8% YoY

On May 9 Glencore announced:

2025 H1 Distribution...Subject to approval by shareholders at the 2025 AGM, it will make a return of capital on 4 June 2025 to holders of record on the Jersey register at the close of business on 2 May 2025. This repayment is of US$0.05 per ordinary share and is to be made in US dollars...

AMG Critical Materials N.V. [NA:AMG] [GR:ADG] (OTCPK:AMVMF) ("AMG")

AMG Vanadium is a leading provider of products and services for the metals, manufacturing, refinery and petrochemical industries. You can read more about AMG Vanadium here.

On May 7 AMG announced:

AMG's portfolio delivers strong First Quarter 2025 results...AMG achieved an adjusted EBITDA of $58 million, an 88% increase compared to the first quarter of 2024 adjusted EBITDA of $31 million...The AMG Technologies segment also had an extremely strong performance...AMG Vanadium's revenue for the first quarter of 2025 decreased by 7%, to $154 million, due primarily to lower volumes of ferrovanadium and titanium alloys.

Note: Q1 2025 Net Income was US$5.023M.

On May 8 AMG announced: "Final 2024 dividend... The final dividend per ordinary share therefore amounts to €0.20."

Largo Inc. [TSX:LGO] [GR:LR81] (LGORF)(NASDAQ:LGO)

Largo Inc. is a pure-play vanadium pentoxide producer from their Maracás Menchen Mine in Brazil as well as a producer of VRFBs.

On May 14, Largo announced:

Largo reports Q1 2025 Financial Results with continued focus on production stability and cost reduction efforts.

  • Revenues of $28.2 million in Q1 2025 vs. 42.2 million in Q1 2024...a result of continued downward pressure in vanadium prices and lower sales volumes.

No news for the month.

VanadiumCorp Resource Inc. [TSXV:VRB][GR:NWN] (OTCPK:VRBFF)

VanadiumCorp Resource Inc. 100% owns the Lac Dore Vanadium-Iron-Titanium Project in Quebec, Canada. The Company is looking to take a vertically integrated approach and is also developing leading process technologies 'VanadiumCorp-Electrochem Processing Technology' and "Electrochem globally patented Electrowinning" technology.

data.title: MPLX LP Remains A Top-Tier Pick
data.description:
Those who know me and who follow my work closely know that I am a big fan of the midstream/pipeline industry. I believe that this is an incredibly stable space that generates significant cash flows. Although many investors are attracted to this industry because of the yields that they can capture, I am more interested in how cheap these companies are compared to the quality that they bring to the table. One of my favorite players in the space has, for a while now, been MPLX LP (NYSE:MPLX). And it's a company that I have had a good track record with.

Back in January of this year, I decided to reaffirm the company as a ‘buy’ candidate. This was based on continued growth that management had achieved, the attractive yield that the company paid out, its low leverage, and how cheap shares were. Since then, the stock has generated upside for investors of 2.1%. That's better than the 3.5% drop experienced by the S&P 500 over the same window of time. But that's nothing. Since I originally rated it a ‘buy’ in September of 2023, shares are up 67%. That dwarfs the 28.7% rise enjoyed by the broader market. Even after all this increase, I maintain that this is a compelling opportunity. Because of this, have no problem keeping the business rated a ‘buy’ right now.

Shares still make sense

Fundamentally speaking, things are going really well for MPLX right now. Take results from the most recent quarter as an example. This would be the first quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. During that time, revenue was $3.12 billion. That represents an increase of 9.8% compared to the 2.85 billion dollars reported one year earlier. This revenue expansion was driven largely by two separate categories of sales. One of these would be its service revenue, which jumped from $1.64 billion to $1.77 billion. And the other would be its product related revenue, which grew from $528 million to $687 million.

The service revenue for the company increased because of higher pipeline throughput that added $57 million to its top line, tariff and other fee escalations associated with crude oil and products logistics that pushed sales up by $24 million, and greater natural gas and NGL volumes and throughput fee rates that added $12 million to its top line. The rise in product revenue, meanwhile, was driven mostly by higher sales volumes in the southwest and Marcellus region that added $91 million in revenue, a $42 million benefit from higher NGL prices attributable to the southwest and Marcellus region, and a $27 million non-recurring benefit associated with a customer agreement that management did not provide much in the way of detail on.

To really understand the increase in sales, however, we should touch on each of its operating segments. During the quarter, the company’s Crude Oil and Products Logistics segment reported sales of $1.59 billion. That's up 4% compared to the $1.53 billion reported the previous year. The increased pipeline throughput and rate escalations that I mentioned already ended up being responsible for this. Total throughput ended up climbing 12% year over year from 5.29 million barrels per day to 5.9 million. Average tariff rates rose from $1.02 per barrel to $1.06 per barrel. The company also enjoyed a 5.6% increase in terminal throughput from 2.93 million barrels per day to 3.10 million barrels per day.

During the quarter, the company also saw growth associated with its Natural Gas and NGL Services segment. Overall revenue jumped 16.5% from $1.32 billion to $1.53 billion. This substantial increase was driven mostly by a 4.7% rise in gathering throughput that includes entities that have been consolidated into the firm, as well as any MPLX-operated equity method investments. Similarly, the amount of natural gas processed jumped 4.4%, while total C2 + NGLs fractionated rose by 4.4%. According to management, during this time, natural gas prices shot up 85.2% from $2.09 per MMBtu to $3.87 per MMBtu.

The rise in revenue has brought with it higher profitability as well. Although net income is not terribly significant in this space, it is worth mentioning that it rose from $995 million to $1.13 billion during this window of time. Operating cash flow fell from $1.29 billion to $1.25 billion. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get an increase from $1.36 billion to $1.48 billion. If we strip out preferred distributions, which I like to strip out because they are required cash payments, we would get a rise from $1.33 billion to $1.47 billion.

Whenever I write about companies in this industry, I also like to look at what I call ‘true free cash flow’. This is adjusted operating cash flow minus maintenance or sustaining capital expenditures. These are capital expenditures needed in order to keep operations running as they are. This ignores growth-oriented capital projects so as to not punish the company for its growth initiatives. Year over year, this metric grew from $1.32 billion to $1.44 billion. If we strip out the preferred distributions, the rise was from $1.29 billion to $1.43 billion. DCF, or distributable cash flow, increased from $1.36 billion to $1.49 billion. And finally, EBITDA for the company expanded from $1.64 billion to $1.76 billion.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for 2023 and 2024 as well. This paints a picture of a company that continues to grow. In all likelihood, 2025 as a whole will be a positive time for the business. If we were to annualize results that we have seen so far for the year, we would end up with the table below. This shows continued expansion for 2025 and is a testament to the initiatives that the company continues to embark on. Of course, the end result here could vary based on acquisitions and the timing of said acquisitions.

On March 11th of this year, for instance, management announced that they had acquired the gathering businesses from Whiptail Midstream LLC in a deal valued at $237 million. These assets primarily consist of crude and natural gas gathering systems. In March of last year, the company used $625 million of the cash that it had on hand to purchase additional ownership interests in existing joint ventures and gathering assets associated with the Utica region. In February of this year, the company entered into an agreement to acquire the remaining 55% ownership interest that it did not own in BANGL, LLC in a deal valued at $715 million, plus additional earnout potential of up to $275 million. Those earnouts will be based on profitability targets between 2026 and 2029. On top of this, the company entered into an agreement to increase its stake in a joint venture that owns and operates the Matterhorn Express pipeline by 5%. That comes at a cost of $151 million and will boost the company's total ownership interest in that business to 10%.

Keeping in mind these uncertainties and additional changes that could occur as time goes on, if we use the estimates for profitability for this year and the historical results from last year, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are currently priced. These are, quite frankly, low multiples for such a high-quality firm. On top of this, in the chart below, I compared MPLX to five similar companies using two of these different valuation metrics. What I found was that, on a price to operating cash flow basis, only one of the five competitors was cheaper than it is. And when it came to the EV to EBITDA approach, two of the five ended up being cheaper.

There are other things besides valuation to take into consideration here. For starters, there is the yield that each of these businesses generates for investors. In the first chart below, you can see the current yield for each firm. With a hefty payout of 7.60%, MPLX is higher than any of the other five companies I compared it to. It's even above my favorite player in the space and a company that I own a significant amount of stock in, Energy Transfer (ET). In the subsequent chart, you can see the net leverage ratio of each of these businesses. This shows that, in addition to having a hefty yield, MPLX has far less net debt than these other companies do relative to the cash flows generated by the business. This means lower risk and greater financial flexibility and should, by definition, translate to a higher price. But as I demonstrated already, MPLX is one of the cheapest players in the space.

As time goes on, investors can expect the firm to grow even more. I already covered some of the investments the business has made leading up to this year. Over 2025 as a whole, management intends to allocate $1.7 billion toward growth initiatives. 85% of this will involve natural gas and NGLs. However, some of it will also be allocated toward the crude oil and products logistics category. Some of the projects that the company has in the works will take some time. As the image below illustrates, it has a number of initiatives coming into service in 2026 and beyond.

Takeaway

Based on the data currently available, I must say that I am a big fan of MPLX. The company is a high quality operator in its space. It is continuing to grow both organically and by means of acquisitions. Shares are cheap compared to most other players. Leverage is super low and its yield is high. Add all of these things together, and I cannot be anything other than bullish about the business. Perhaps the only downside is that there is risk associated with its largest shareholder. This is none other than Marathon Petroleum Corporation (MPC), which has a 64% ownership interest in common units representing limited partner interests. More importantly, that shareholder also accounts for a large portion of the company's revenue and other income. Last year, in fact, this amounted to 49%. There is always a risk caused by such a high concentration of revenue and, in turn, profits. But for those who feel comfortable with this arrangement, I believe that a very solid ‘buy’ rating is appropriate.

In terms of individual stocks that positively contributed, BAE Systems (OTCPK:BAESF)(OTCPK:BAESY), a UK-based defence company, looks well positioned to benefit from increased defence spending by EU countries as the region seeks to reduce its reliance on the US. Shares of Engie (OTCPK:ENGIY)(OTCPK:ENGQF), a diversified European utility company, rose after the company guided to better-than-expected medium-term profitability targets. ING Groep (ING), a Dutch bank with operations across Europe, participated in a broad-based rise in bank shares as investors grew more optimistic about Europe’s macroeconomic outlook and its potential to support bank earnings. Not owning companies such as Amazon.com (AMZN), Tesla (TSLA) and Meta Platforms (META) also proved beneficial. The former two do not pay dividends, while the latter offers only a minimal yield, which is why they are not held in the portfolio.

In terms of individual stock detractors, shares of Target (TGT) sold off during the period amid declining consumer sentiment and fears that extremely high reciprocal tariffs on Chinese imports could hurt its business if not reduced to a more manageable level. Broadcom (AVGO) is a key supplier of graphics processing units for generative AI and sold off on investor concerns about future demand for AI chips. Blackstone (BX), a preeminent alternative asset manager, saw its stock come under pressure after the hoped-for improvement in the initial public offering market failed to materialise in early 2025, and as the risk of a full-blown trade war reduced expectations for the crystallisation of future performance fees.

Market review

Global equity markets ended lower over the period, weighed down by mounting concerns over the economic implications of US President Trump’s tariff policies. These fuelled fears of a global trade war, with early signs that tariff-driven inflationary pressures were eroding consumer confidence and could slow US economic growth. In April, markets sold off sharply after the announcement of sweeping new US tariffs – including a 10% blanket duty on all imports and higher rates on specific countries. However, equities recovered later in the month after the US announced a 90-day pause on most of the measures as part of broader trade negotiations, easing some of the immediate pressure on markets. Sharply increased duties on Chinese imports remained in place, though, intensifying tensions between the two countries. At the same time, persistent inflation raised concerns that the US Federal Reserve (Fed) could slow the pace of interest-rate cuts, despite signs of weakening growth, given its relatively hawkish stance. President Trump also sought to calm investors by affirming that the Fed’s independence was not at risk. Meanwhile, geopolitical tensions remained elevated, with US–Russia peace talks over the three-year Ukraine conflict failing to deliver a permanent ceasefire. Elsewhere, the German government’s announcement of sizeable fiscal stimulus measures for defence and infrastructure provided a rare source of positive policy news in the eurozone.

US equities ended notably lower. Despite falling bond yields and exposure to the fast-growing artificial intelligence sector, large technology companies significantly underperformed the broader index, as investors became more risk-averse and concerned about the competitive threat posed by Chinese start-up DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK). Annualised GDP unexpectedly contracted by 0.3% in the first quarter, according to an advance estimate, after growing by 2.4% in the fourth quarter. The decline primarily reflected a surge in imports ahead of the new tariffs, alongside softer consumer spending. The Fed’s preferred inflation measure – the annual core Personal Consumption Expenditures Price Index – fell from 3.0% in February to 2.6% in March, as expected, but remained above its 2% target. The Fed kept the target range for the federal funds rate at 4.25–4.50% over the period, also in line with expectations.

European equities fell over the period. Eurozone GDP grew by a higher-than-expected 0.4% sequentially in the first quarter, according to a flash estimate, after rising by 0.2% in the last quarter of 2024. Meanwhile, a flash estimate showed that annual consumer price inflation remained at 2.2% in April, while the core rate rose from 2.4% in March to 2.7%, which was above expectations. The ECB cut the deposit facility rate by 25 bps at both its March and April meetings, taking it to 2.25%.

UK equities ended lower over the period. Chancellor Rachel Reeves announced £14 billion of spending cuts in the Spring Statement to help improve the UK’s fiscal position. The UK economy unexpectedly expanded by 0.1% sequentially in the fourth quarter after stalling in the prior three months, while annual growth increased from 1.2% to a higher-than-expected 1.5%. Annual consumer price inflation declined from 2.8% in February to a lower-than-expected 2.6% in March, though it remained above the Bank of England (BoE)’s 2% target. The BoE lowered its Bank Rate by 25 bps to 4.50% in February.

Outlook & strategy

Macroeconomic factors remain as unpredictable as ever. The trajectory of the US and global economies is dependent on the outcome of trade and tariff negotiations in response to President Trump’s “Liberation Day” reciprocal tariff announcements on 2 April. While a soft landing appeared to be the most likely outcome prior to the announcement, the risk of a recession in the US and/or globally is increasing, especially if countries choose to retaliate rather than negotiate. Nevertheless, our focus remains at the stock level, ensuring the portfolio is well diversified on both a regional and sectoral basis, and robust enough to preserve capital in periods of market weakness.
